does: This paper proposes a modification to sparse autoencoders (SAEs) by adding a small rank-r linear bottleneck in parallel with standard SAE architectures like BatchTopK and Matryoshka. The core contribution is identifying that a low-rank, dense component of activations is computationally important yet unsuitable for sparse representation, causing the dense latents persistently observed in trained SAEs. The authors demonstrate on Gemma-2-2B that a rank-24 bottleneck reduces dense latent counts by up to 84% while improving sparse probing and targeted perturbation performance at matched sparsity. They show the absorbed component is both structurally identifiable as top principal components and causally necessary for model function.
connects: This work builds directly on recent research establishing that SAEs can extract interpretable, monosemantic features from neural networks, particularly addressing limitations of earlier SAE approaches. It responds to an observed practical problem in SAE training: the emergence of dense latents that resist sparse decomposition despite theoretical motivation for sparsity. The paper engages with principal component analysis traditions in neural network analysis and extends work questioning whether all activation structure should be decomposed sparsely. It combines insights from interpretability research on feature extraction with causal methods for validating learned representations, building on established techniques for measuring model functionality.
enables: This approach opens directions for more efficient sparse feature extraction by separating fundamentally dense from sparse structure in activations, potentially improving the interpretability and computational efficiency of SAE-based analysis. The work suggests follow-up research into characterizing which components across different models and layers are inherently dense versus sparse, enabling better architecture design for feature extraction. It points toward hybrid decomposition strategies that could scale to larger models by respecting the actual structure of activations rather than forcing all content into sparse dictionaries. The causal validation methodology established here—measuring necessity through perturbation—could extend to evaluating other interpretability methods and designing better mechanistic understanding tools for neural networks.
